section.title: The Death of Ahab
section.sectionRationale: After three years of peace, Ahab persuades Jehoshaphat of Judah to join him against Aram at Ramoth-gilead. Four hundred court prophets promise victory in unison, but Jehoshaphat presses for a true prophet of the LORD, and the reluctantly summoned Micaiah reveals a startling throne room vision: the LORD has sent a lying spirit into the mouths of Ahab's prophets to entice him to his doom. Struck, imprisoned, and ignored, Micaiah's word nonetheless comes to pass exactly: disguised in battle, Ahab is struck by a stray arrow and dies as his blood is licked by dogs, just as Elijah had foretold. The chapter closes with brief, contrasting notices of the reigns of Jehoshaphat of Judah and Ahaziah of Israel.
section.thesis: Surrounded by four hundred prophets who tell him what he wants to hear, Ahab cannot escape the one true word of the LORD spoken through Micaiah, and even a lying spirit permitted by God to entice him to battle cannot prevent, but only fulfills, the exact judgment already pronounced against him.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Ahab and Jehoshaphat's alliance and the four hundred false prophets, verses 1 to 12; Micaiah's true word and the lying spirit, verses 13 to 28; the death of Ahab exactly as foretold, verses 29 to 40; Jehoshaphat's reign in Judah, verses 41 to 50; and Ahaziah's reign in Israel, verses 51 to 53.

units.label: Ahab and the false prophets
units.note: four hundred voices tell the king what he wants

verses.text: Then three years passed without war between Aram and Israel.
However, in the third year, Jehoshaphat king of Judah went down to visit the king of Israel,
who said to his servants, “Do you not know that Ramoth-gilead is ours, but we have failed to take it from the hand of the king of Aram?”
So he asked Jehoshaphat, “Will you go with me to fight against Ramoth-gilead?” Jehoshaphat answered the king of Israel, “I am as you are, my people are your people, and my horses are your horses.”
But Jehoshaphat also said to the king of Israel, “Please inquire first for the {{dabar|word}} of the LORD.”

So the king of Israel assembled the {{navi|prophets}}, about four hundred men, and asked them, “Should I go to war against Ramoth-gilead, or should I refrain?” “Go up,” they replied, “and the Lord will deliver it into the hand of the king.”
But Jehoshaphat asked, “Is there not still a prophet of the LORD here of whom we can inquire?”

The king of Israel answered, “There is still one man through whom we can inquire of the LORD, but I hate him because he never prophesies anything good for me, but only bad. He is Micaiah son of Imlah.” “The king should not say that!” Jehoshaphat replied.
So the king of Israel called one of his officials and said, “Bring Micaiah son of Imlah at once.”

Dressed in royal attire, the king of Israel and Jehoshaphat king of Judah were sitting on their thrones at the threshing floor by the entrance of the gate of Samaria, with all the prophets prophesying before them.
Now Zedekiah son of Chenaanah had made for himself iron horns and declared, “This is what the LORD says: ‘With these you shall gore the Arameans until they are finished off.’”
And all the prophets were prophesying the same, saying, “Go up to Ramoth-gilead and triumph, for the LORD will deliver it into the hand of the king.”

units.label: Micaiah's true word and the lying spirit
units.note: one true prophet against four hundred

verses.text: Then the messenger who had gone to call Micaiah instructed him, “Behold now, with one accord the words of the prophets are favorable to the king. So please let your words be like theirs, and speak favorably.”
But Micaiah said, “As surely as the LORD lives, I will speak whatever the LORD tells me.”

When Micaiah arrived, the king asked him, “Micaiah, should we go to war against Ramoth-gilead, or should we refrain?” “Go up and triumph,” Micaiah replied, “for the LORD will deliver it into the hand of the king.”
But the king said to him, “How many times must I make you swear not to tell me anything but the truth in the name of the LORD?”
So Micaiah declared: “I saw all Israel scattered on the hills like sheep without a shepherd. And the LORD said, ‘These people have no master; let each one return home in peace.’”
Then the king of Israel said to Jehoshaphat, “Did I not tell you that he never prophesies good for me, but only bad?”
Micaiah continued, “Therefore hear the word of the LORD: I saw the LORD sitting on His throne, and all the host of heaven standing by Him on His right and on His left.
And the LORD said, ‘Who will entice Ahab to march up and fall at Ramoth-gilead?’ And one suggested this, and another that.
Then a spirit came forward, stood before the LORD, and said, ‘I will entice him.’ ‘By what means?’ asked the LORD.
And he replied, ‘I will go out and be a {{ruachsheqer|lying spirit}} in the mouths of all his prophets.’ ‘You will surely entice him and prevail,’ said the LORD. ‘Go and do it.’
So you see, the LORD has put a lying spirit in the mouths of all these prophets of yours, and the LORD has pronounced disaster against you.”
Then Zedekiah son of Chenaanah went up, struck Micaiah in the face, and demanded, “Which way did the Spirit of the LORD go when He departed from me to speak with you?”
Micaiah replied, “You will soon see, on that day when you go and hide in an inner room.”
And the king of Israel declared, “Take Micaiah and return him to Amon the governor of the city and to Joash the king’s son,
and tell them that this is what the king says: ‘Put this man in prison and feed him only bread and water until I return safely.’”
But Micaiah replied, “If you ever return safely, the LORD has not spoken through me.” Then he added, “Take heed, all you people!”

units.label: The death of Ahab at Ramoth-gilead

verses.text: So the king of Israel and Jehoshaphat king of Judah went up to Ramoth-gilead.
And the king of Israel said to Jehoshaphat, “I will disguise myself and go into battle, but you wear your royal robes.” So the king of Israel disguised himself and went into battle.
Now the king of Aram had ordered his thirty-two chariot commanders, “Do not fight with anyone, small or great, except the king of Israel.”
When the chariot commanders saw Jehoshaphat, they said, “Surely this is the king of Israel!” So they turned to fight against him, but Jehoshaphat cried out.
And when the chariot commanders saw that he was not the king of Israel, they turned back from pursuing him.

However, a certain man drew his bow without taking special aim, and he struck the king of Israel between the joints of his armor. So the king said to his charioteer, “Turn around and take me out of the battle, for I am badly wounded!”
The battle raged throughout that day, and the king was propped up in his chariot facing the Arameans. And the blood from his wound ran out onto the floor of the chariot, and that evening he died.
As the sun was setting, the cry rang out in the army: “Every man to his own city, and every man to his own land!”
So the king died and was brought to Samaria, where they buried him.
And the chariot was washed at the pool of Samaria where the prostitutes bathed, and the dogs licked up Ahab’s blood, according to the word that the LORD had spoken.
As for the rest of the acts of Ahab, along with all his accomplishments and the ivory palace and all the cities he built, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
And Ahab rested with his fathers, and his son Ahaziah reigned in his place.
In the fourth year of Ahab’s reign over Israel, Jehoshaphat son of Asa became king of Judah.
Jehoshaphat was thirty-five years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem twenty-five years. His mother’s name was Azubah daughter of Shilhi.

And Jehoshaphat walked in all the ways of his father Asa; he did not turn away from them, but did what was right in the eyes of the LORD. The high places, however, were not removed; the people still sacrificed and burned incense on the high places.
Jehoshaphat also made peace with the king of Israel.
As for the rest of the acts of Jehoshaphat, along with the might he exercised and how he waged war, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
He banished from the land the male shrine prostitutes who remained from the days of his father Asa.
And there was no king in Edom; a deputy served as king.
Jehoshaphat built ships of Tarshish to go to Ophir for gold, but they never set sail, because they were wrecked at Ezion-geber.
At that time Ahaziah son of Ahab said to Jehoshaphat, “Let my servants sail with your servants,” but Jehoshaphat refused.
And Jehoshaphat rested with his fathers and was buried with them in the city of his father David. And his son Jehoram reigned in his place.
In the seventeenth year of Jehoshaphat’s reign over Judah, Ahaziah son of Ahab became king of Israel, and he reigned in Samaria two years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD and walked in the ways of his father and mother and of Jeroboam son of Nebat, who had caused Israel to sin.
Ahaziah served and worshiped Baal, provoking the LORD, the God of Israel, to anger, just as his father had done.

navi.gloss: Prophet. Four hundred court nevi'im tell Ahab exactly what he wants to hear, while Micaiah, the one true navi Ahab admits he hates 'because he never prophesies anything good for me,' speaks only what the LORD actually says. The chapter contrasts a crowd of professional prophets with a single faithful one.

dabar.gloss: Word. Jehoshaphat's first instinct is to ask for the dabar of the LORD rather than trust the chorus of court prophets, and Micaiah swears he will speak only the dabar the LORD gives him, whatever the cost, showing that the true measure of a prophet is fidelity to God's actual word, not popularity or comfort.

ruachsheqer.gloss: A lying spirit. In Micaiah's throne room vision, a spirit volunteers to entice Ahab by becoming a ruach sheqer in the mouths of all his prophets, and the LORD grants permission, 'go and do it.' The scene affirms God's sovereignty even over deception used in judgment, while never making Him the author of the lie itself, since the false prophets remain responsible for the falsehood they speak.

basic.context: After three years without war, Ahab persuades Jehoshaphat of Judah to join him in retaking Ramoth-gilead from Aram. Jehoshaphat asks first for a true word from the LORD, so four hundred prophets unanimously promise victory, until Jehoshaphat presses for one more, Micaiah, whom Ahab admits he hates because Micaiah never prophesies good for him. Micaiah first mocks the king with false agreement, then, pressed to tell the truth, describes a vision of the LORD's throne room in which a spirit volunteers to become a lying spirit in the mouths of Ahab's prophets to entice him to his death at Ramoth-gilead. Struck in the face and imprisoned for his honesty, Micaiah's word nonetheless comes true to the letter: Ahab disguises himself in battle, but a stray arrow finds him anyway, and he dies exactly as Elijah and Micaiah had said. The chapter closes with brief notices of Jehoshaphat's generally faithful reign in Judah and Ahaziah's wicked reign in Israel.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a specific war council, a specific prophetic contest, and a specific fulfillment of earlier judgment; it is not a manual on how to seek guidance, though it does illustrate true versus false prophecy. The lying spirit episode, verses 19-23, requires careful handling: it affirms God's sovereign rule even over deceptive spirits used in judgment on someone already under sentence, while the moral responsibility for the lie remains with the spirit and the false prophets who speak it.
basic.originalLanguages: Jehoshaphat's first request is for the dabar, word, of the LORD, in deliberate contrast to the four hundred nevi'im, prophets, who tell Ahab only what he wants to hear. In Micaiah's vision, a spirit offers to become a ruach sheqer, a lying spirit, in the mouths of those very prophets, and the LORD permits it.
basic.typology: Micaiah, hated for speaking only what the LORD actually says rather than what the king wants to hear, and struck for his faithfulness, anticipates Christ, who was likewise hated and struck for speaking the Father's truth rather than pleasing His hearers.
basic.christ: Micaiah stands utterly alone, one true word against four hundred flattering voices, and is struck for his faithfulness. Jesus is the greater, final Prophet who likewise speaks only what the Father gives Him, John 12:49-50, and is struck on the face by those He came to warn, Matthew 26:67-68.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over the mechanism of judgment, permitting a lying spirit to entice a king who has already forfeited the right to any further warning, while still ensuring, through Micaiah, that Ahab hears the full truth and chooses to ignore it. Every detail Elijah and Micaiah foretold comes to pass exactly.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 21:19 supplies the earlier sentence fulfilled here exactly. Deuteronomy 18 gives the test of a true prophet, that his word comes to pass. John 12 shows Jesus speaking only what the Father commands. And Matthew 26 shows Jesus struck for speaking truth, echoing Micaiah's treatment.
basic.oneStory: A king surrounded by four hundred voices telling him what he wants to hear cannot escape the single true word of the LORD spoken through one faithful, unpopular prophet. Ahab dies exactly as foretold, down to the last detail, a sober reminder that God's word never fails, pointing forward to Christ, the true and final Prophet.

context.literary: The chapter is carefully structured around contrast: four hundred voices against one, royal robes against a disguise, a king's confident question against a shepherd's ominous vision, and finally a random arrow against a precisely fulfilled prophetic word, all building toward the exact fulfillment of judgment first announced back in chapter 21.
context.historical: Ramoth-gilead, a fortified city east of the Jordan, was a strategically important border town repeatedly contested between Israel and Aram; disguising oneself in battle while a co-belligerent wore royal regalia was a known tactic to divert enemy targeting, though it ultimately fails Ahab here. Sitting at the threshing floor by the city gate, verse 10, was the customary setting for royal audiences and official proceedings.
context.canonical: This chapter closes the Ahab narrative begun in 1 Kings 16, bringing to precise fulfillment the sentence pronounced in 1 Kings 21:19, and it hands the story forward into 2 Kings through the brief notice of Ahaziah's evil reign, while Jehoshaphat's account continues more fully in 2 Chronicles 17-20.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To contrast the crowd of flattering court prophets with the single faithful prophet of the LORD, to show that God's earlier word of judgment against Ahab comes to precise fulfillment despite Ahab's attempts to evade it, and to close the era of Ahab's reign with a sober accounting alongside the more faithful reign of Jehoshaphat.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific prophetic contest and a specific act of divine judgment through a lying spirit permitted against a king already under sentence; it is not a general statement that God normally deceives people, nor a method for testing prophets beyond the enduring principle already given in Deuteronomy 18, that a true word from the LORD comes to pass. What endures is the call to seek and heed God's true word even when it is unwelcome, and the certainty that His word, once spoken, will be fulfilled.

debates.question: How can God be just while sending, or permitting, a lying spirit to entice Ahab, verses 19-23?

views.label: Judicial deception permitted against a king already under sentence
views.case: Ahab has already received Elijah's verdict in chapter 21 and has repeatedly rejected true prophetic warning throughout his reign, including in chapter 20; the LORD's permission of a lying spirit here executes rather than originates his judgment, similar to how God gives people over to a delusion they have already chosen, 2 Thessalonians 2:10-12.

views.label: A vivid, dramatic depiction of God's sovereignty over all spiritual powers, including deceptive ones
views.case: The scene is cast as a throne room vision using the genre of ancient court imagery; its main point is not a literal transaction but an emphatic affirmation that no power, not even a lying spirit, operates outside the LORD's sovereign permission and purpose, however that permission is mechanically pictured.

debates.bearing: Both readings preserve God's justice: Ahab receives, through Micaiah, the full truth and freely rejects it before any lying spirit is described as acting, so the judgment falls on a king who has already hardened himself against a clear and repeated true word.

debates.question: Why does Micaiah first answer sarcastically, 'Go up and triumph,' verse 15, before giving his true word?

views.label: Mocking irony exposing the absurdity of expecting him to lie
views.case: Micaiah echoes the exact words of the false prophets in a tone Ahab immediately recognizes as insincere, since the king presses him, 'how many times must I make you swear,' verse 16, showing the first answer was understood by both men as sarcasm rather than genuine prophecy.

views.label: A deliberate test to force the king to demand the truth openly
views.case: By initially giving the answer everyone wants to hear, Micaiah puts the responsibility on Ahab to explicitly ask for the LORD's true word rather than simply receiving flattery he did not really want challenged, exposing that Ahab already suspected the truth and needed to ask for it himself.

debates.bearing: Either way, the exchange shows that Ahab is never left without a genuine opportunity to hear and heed the LORD's true word, undercutting any claim that he was simply deceived without recourse.

study.originalLanguages: Jehoshaphat's first request is for the dabar, word, of the LORD, in deliberate contrast to the four hundred nevi'im, prophets, who tell Ahab only what he wants to hear. In Micaiah's throne room vision, a spirit offers to become a ruach sheqer, a lying spirit, in the mouths of those very prophets, and the LORD permits it, since Ahab, already under Elijah's earlier sentence, has forfeited the right to a true word he refuses to heed anyway. Micaiah himself swears he will speak only the dabar the LORD gives him, whatever the cost, embodying the very fidelity to God's word that the four hundred lack.

typology.type: Micaiah, the one true prophet hated and struck for refusing to tell the king comfortable lies
typology.fulfillment: Christ, hated and struck for speaking only the Father's truth, John 7:7, Matthew 26:67-68.

typology.type: The precise fulfillment of the prophetic word against Ahab, down to the smallest detail
typology.fulfillment: God's word never returns void, Isaiah 55:11, ultimately and supremely fulfilled in the exact accomplishment of every prophecy concerning Christ's death and resurrection.

typology.type: A king who disguises himself yet cannot escape the judgment already pronounced against him
typology.fulfillment: The certainty that no one can hide from God's righteous judgment except by taking refuge in Christ, who bore that judgment in the place of His people, Hebrews 4:13.

study.christ: Micaiah stands utterly alone, one true word against four hundred voices telling the king exactly what he wants to hear, and he is struck in the face and imprisoned for his faithfulness, verses 24-27. Jesus is the greater, final Prophet who likewise speaks only what the Father gives Him, John 12:49-50, is hated by those who prefer comfortable lies, John 3:19-20, and is struck on the face and condemned by those He came to warn, Matthew 26:67-68. Where Ahab chooses four hundred flatterers over one true prophet and dies exactly as warned, all who reject Christ's true word likewise face the very judgment He came to warn them of, while all who receive it are saved.
study.god: God is sovereign even over the mechanism of judgment, permitting a lying spirit to entice a king who has already, through his own persistent unbelief, forfeited the right to any further warning, while still ensuring, through Micaiah, that Ahab hears the full truth and chooses to ignore it. God's word, once spoken, cannot fail. Every detail Elijah pronounced in the vineyard, and every detail Micaiah foresaw, comes to pass exactly, down to the stray arrow and the dogs at the pool of Samaria. And God remains just even in the closing genealogical notices, distinguishing Jehoshaphat's largely faithful reign from Ahaziah's continuation of his parents' evil.

crossReferences.note: Elijah's earlier sentence that dogs would lick Ahab's own blood is fulfilled precisely in 22:38.
The test of a true prophet is whether his word comes to pass; Micaiah's does, exactly, while the four hundred are proven false.
Jesus, like Micaiah, testifies that He speaks only what the Father has commanded Him to say, regardless of the cost.
Jesus, the true Prophet, is struck in the face and mocked for speaking the truth, echoing Micaiah's treatment at Zedekiah's hand.

study.oneStory: A king surrounded by four hundred voices telling him what he wants to hear cannot escape the single true word of the LORD spoken through one faithful, unpopular prophet. Even the LORD's sovereign permission of a lying spirit only fulfills, rather than replaces, the judgment already announced through Elijah, and Ahab dies exactly as foretold, down to the last detail. The chapter is a sober reminder that God's word never fails, whether it comes as warning or as judgment, and it points forward to Christ, the true and final Prophet, hated and struck for speaking truth, whose word likewise never fails.

quiz.q: What did Jehoshaphat ask for before agreeing to join Ahab's war against Aram, verse 5?

options.text: He asked Ahab to first inquire for the word of the LORD, rather than relying only on political alliance.
options.note: Verse 5 records his exact request.

options.text: He asked for a larger share of the plunder from Ramoth-gilead.
options.note: No such request appears anywhere in the text.

options.text: He asked Ahab to delay the war until the following year.
options.note: No delay is requested; Jehoshaphat asks for a prophetic word, not a postponement.

options.text: He refused outright to join Ahab in any military campaign.
options.note: Jehoshaphat had already pledged his people and horses to Ahab in verse 4, and he does go to battle in verse 29.

quiz.q: How did the four hundred prophets differ from Micaiah, verses 6-14?

options.text: The four hundred unanimously told Ahab exactly what he wanted to hear, while Micaiah, whom Ahab admitted hating, insisted he would speak only what the LORD actually told him.
options.note: Verses 6-14 draw exactly this contrast, including Ahab's own admission in verse 8.

options.text: The four hundred were foreign prophets who did not know the LORD's name at all.
options.note: The text has them speaking in the LORD's name, verse 6, so this overstates their foreignness.

options.text: Micaiah was younger and less experienced than the other prophets.
options.note: No such detail about age or experience appears in the text.

options.text: All four hundred prophets and Micaiah agreed completely on the outcome.
options.note: Micaiah's true word directly contradicts the unanimous favorable message of the four hundred.

quiz.q: What did Micaiah see in his vision of the LORD's throne room, verses 19-22?

options.text: The LORD asked who would entice Ahab to his death at Ramoth-gilead, and a spirit volunteered to become a lying spirit in the mouths of all Ahab's prophets.
options.note: Verses 19-22 describe exactly this throne room exchange.

options.text: He saw Ahab already victorious and celebrating in Samaria.
options.note: This is the opposite of the vision, which foresees Ahab's death, not a celebration of victory.

options.text: He saw all Israel gathered safely at peace under Ahab's rule forever.
options.note: Verse 17 actually shows Israel scattered like sheep without a shepherd, signifying Ahab would die, not that peace would continue.

options.text: He saw nothing unusual and simply repeated what the other prophets said.
options.note: Micaiah's vision is strikingly different from, and directly contradicts, the message of the other prophets.

quiz.q: How did Ahab try to avoid the fate Micaiah predicted, and what happened anyway, verses 30-34?

options.text: He disguised himself in battle while Jehoshaphat wore royal robes, yet a random arrow struck him between the joints of his armor and he died that evening.
options.note: Verses 30-35 describe exactly this attempted evasion and its failure.

options.text: He avoided the battle entirely by sending Jehoshaphat in his place.
options.note: Ahab does go into battle himself, disguised, rather than avoiding it entirely.

options.text: He wore extra armor for full protection and was completely unharmed.
options.note: The arrow struck him precisely between the joints of his armor, wounding him fatally.

options.text: He was captured alive by the Arameans and held for ransom.
options.note: Ahab is wounded and dies in his own chariot; he is not captured.

quiz.q: How was Elijah's earlier prophecy against Ahab, 1 Kings 21:19, fulfilled at his death, verse 38?

options.text: Ahab's chariot was washed at the pool of Samaria and dogs licked up his blood, exactly as the LORD had spoken through Elijah.
options.note: Verse 38 explicitly ties this event to the word the LORD had spoken.

options.text: Ahab died in battle and was given a great public funeral with no further detail noted.
options.note: Ahab is buried, verse 37, but the text specifically also records the dogs licking his blood, the precise detail that fulfills Elijah's word.

options.text: Ahaziah, not Ahab, was the one whose blood was licked by dogs.
options.note: The text is explicit that it was Ahab's own blood, matching the sentence pronounced against Ahab himself.

options.text: The prophecy failed, since Ahab died peacefully in his own bed.
options.note: Ahab dies from a battle wound, and the text affirms the prophecy's fulfillment rather than its failure.

quiz.q: How do the closing notices of Jehoshaphat and Ahaziah, verses 41-53, contrast?

options.text: Jehoshaphat did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, though the high places remained, while Ahaziah did evil, following the ways of Ahab, Jezebel, and Jeroboam, and served Baal.
options.note: Verses 43 and 52-53 state exactly this contrast.

options.text: Both kings are commended equally as faithful followers of the LORD.
options.note: Ahaziah is explicitly said to have done evil and served Baal, unlike Jehoshaphat.

options.text: Jehoshaphat is condemned for the same sins as Ahab and Jezebel.
options.note: The text instead commends Jehoshaphat for doing what was right, unlike Ahab's house.

options.text: Neither king's reign is described at all in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 41-53 give specific descriptions of both reigns.

questions: Ahab surrounded himself with four hundred prophets who told him only what he wanted to hear. Where in your life do you surround yourself with voices that flatter rather than voices that tell you the truth?

Jehoshaphat's first instinct was to ask, 'Is there not a prophet of the LORD here?' even after hearing four hundred prophets agree. What helps you keep seeking God's actual word rather than settling for consensus or comfortable opinion?
Micaiah spoke the truth knowing it would cost him a slap in the face and imprisonment. Where is God calling you to speak an unwelcome truth regardless of the personal cost?

Ahab disguised himself to escape the fate he had been warned of, yet a stray arrow found him anyway. Where might you be trying to outmaneuver a consequence rather than dealing honestly with the warning God has already given you?
Every detail Elijah and Micaiah foretold about Ahab's death came true exactly. How does the certainty of God's word, whether promise or warning, shape the way you take His word seriously today?

Jehoshaphat's reign is described as largely faithful, yet the high places were never removed. Where in your own life is there unfinished obedience, good in general direction but incomplete in the details?

translationNotes.note: Sitting at 'the threshing floor by the entrance of the gate of Samaria' places the scene at the customary site for royal audiences and legal proceedings, underscoring the public, official nature of this prophetic consultation.

Some manuscripts divide verses 21 and 22 differently, as the BSB footnote notes, without affecting the substance of the vision: a spirit volunteers, and the LORD grants permission for it to become a lying spirit in the prophets' mouths.
'Turn around' renders a Hebrew idiom literally meaning 'turn your hand,' the charioteer's action of wheeling the chariot out of the fighting.
The BSB footnote allows 'where the prostitutes bathed' to be rendered 'where they cleaned the weapons'; either way the verse explicitly ties the scene back to Elijah's word in 1 Kings 21:19.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 22:1-53. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced elsewhere in this file.
